segments.text:
Chapter 15 part II
Cardiovascular system
Chuck Norris frequently donates blood to the red cross…just never his own.

Heart output
CO = SV x HR
Cardiac output = stroke volume x heart rate
(for example; if the stroke volume is 70mL and the HR is 72 bpm then the cardiac output is 5,040 mL per minute)
Cardiac output is always = to metabolic demand (cellular needs) of the body…if not then we have problems.
Frank Starling Law- CO is equal to venous return. The more blood coming back into the heart the greater the distension of the ventricles thus more mmHg to push out the blood.

Regulation of cardiac cycle
The volume of blood pumped will constantly change to accommodate cellular requirements.
when a person is at rest the heart only pumps 4 to 6 liters of blood each minute; during strenuous exercise the heart may be required to pump four to seven times this amount

Regulation of Cardiac Output
Our SA node usually controls the rate at which the heart beats, but perhaps you need the heart to beat slower (put the breaks on) or faster and harder. In these cases you need to have other inputs.
Parasympathetic – nerve fibers starting in the medulla oblongata contribute to the vagus nerve. The vagus nerves secrete ACh and can slow the heart rate. Rt vagus leads to SA node, left vagus leads to AV node, both lead to other cardiac target cells. More parasympathetic activity = slower heart; less parasympathetic activity allows the heart to beat closer to the SA nodes normal activty (about 80 bpm )
Sympathetic – sympathetic stimulation comes from accelerator nerves arising from ganglia supplied by T1-T4(T5). The accelerator nerves secrete NE ( norepinephrine ) to increase heart rate and heart contractility and also dilates coronary arteries.
Autonomic fxn is regulated in this case by the cardiac control center of the medulla
sympathetics
Control of Cardiac Output

Control of the Heart
Baroreceptos – baroreceptor reflexes are housed in the cardiac control center of the medulla oblongata. We can sense the amount of pressures in the arteries…too much pressure the heart will be told to slow down ( vagus ) to decrease pressure
(or if too much venous mmHg is detected in the vena cava it can speed up)
Too little pressure in the arteries stimulate veins to add blood volume to the system and signal kidneys to reserve water (also thirst)
Chemoreceptors – can sense the amount of O2 and CO2 in the blood (along with the pH). Too much or too little of these chemicals will also alter heart productivity. E.g. Too little blood oxygen the heart will be forced to work harder to send freshly oxygenated blood to the tissues in order to meet metabolic demand.
http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0073525707/student_view0/chapter15/baroreceptor_reflex_control_of_blood_pressure.html [http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0073525707/student_view0/chapter15/baroreceptor_reflex_control_of_blood_pressure.html]
http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0072943696/student_view0/chapter13/animation__chemoreceptor_reflex_control_of_blood_pressure.html [http://highered.mcgraw-hill.com/sites/0072943696/student_view0/chapter13/animation__chemoreceptor_reflex_control_of_blood_pressure.html]

Other factors that can effect  heart rate
Temperature – fever (pyrexia) and hyperthermia raises the heart rate
cold temp – reduces it
Emotion – anxiety can increase heart rate. In such cases the cerebrum and hypothalmus may become involved and influence the cardiac control centers.
Drugs – stimulants increase HR
depressants decrease HR
Ion concentration- one major ion in the blood that can strongly affect the hearts ability to work is calcium . In cardiac muscle tissue the sarcoplasmic reticula is less developed than in skeletal muscle. The heart is therefore more dependent on extracellular (blood- bourne ) calcium levels for part of its contraction process.
Another ion of importance is potassium . As we learned before, potassium levels can alter the electrical potential of cell membranes.
In a nutshell:
Too much potassium ( hyperkalemia ) – decrease heart rate and contraction, may lead to cardiac arrest if too high
Too low potassium ( hypokalemia )- can lead to arrythmias
Too much calcium ( hypercalcemia ) – can increase heart action and may lead to prolonged contractions
Too little calcium ( hypocalcemia )- can depress heart action, heart will have difficulty contracting.

Age also affects heart rate
Age Range Average Rate:
0-1 month 	100-180
2-3 months 	110-180
4-12 months 	80-180
1-3 years 		80-160 (130)
4-5 years 		80-120 (100)
6-8 years 		70-115 (100)
9-11 years 	60-110 (88)
12-16 years 	60-110 (80)
>16 years 	50-90 (70)

CO = SV x HR
We have just briefly gone over a few control mechanisms for how we regulate CO. We will need to further discuss this topic and how it also deals with blood pressure, but first we need to understand the vessels.

Blood vessels: Arteries, Veins, and Capillaries
Arteries are strong, elastic vessels adapted for carrying blood AWAY from the heart. It is composed of 3 layers :
Innermost layer is called tunica intima , which is composed of simple squamous epithelium, called endothelium, resting on a layer of elastic and collagenous fibers. This inner layer should be very smooth to allow easy blood flow
Tunica media is the middle layer and makes up the bulk of the arterial wall. It is made up of elastic tissue and smooth muscle which can withstand the forces of blood mmHg and ventricular pumping
Tunica externa (adventitia) is the outermost layer and is made up of connective tissue (again elastic and collagenous but more irregular is composition).
Endothelial effects on blood flow- again, the endothelium should provide a smooth surface for blood and platelets to easily pass through.  The endothelium, itself can also help regulate blood flow by secreting substances (e.g. nitric oxide) that either dilate or constrict blood vessels (by allowing contraction or relaxation of the surrounding smooth muscles in the arterial walls).

Blood vessels: Arteries, Veins, and Capillaries
Veins - formed by venules which arise at the terminal portions of the capillaries. Veins carry blood BACK to the heart. Similar to arteries, veins have 3 layers but the middle layer is not nearly as developed as in the artery. One unique feature that veins have that we do not see in arteries is the presence of valves …which prevent backflow. Valves only permit one-way flow of blood.
Capillaries - smallest diameter blood vessels. They connect the smallest arterioles (tiny branches of arteries) with the smallest venules . Capillaries are extensions of the inner layer of an artery, made up of simple squamous epithelium, and are the sites of nutrient and waste exchange b/w blood and tissues. The areas of the body with the highest capillary concentrations are those with the highest metabolism (e.g. in muscle and nerve tissue). Areas with little metabolism going on require few capillaries (e.g. cartilage).
metaarterioles - metaarterioles are branches off of the microscopic arterioles. These metaarterioles go into the capillary beds or bypass them and directly connect to venules . If the metaarteriole bypasses the capillary to go directly into the venous system we call these arteriovenous shunts.

Vasoconstriction and vasodilation of arteries
The sympathetic branches of the autonomic nervous system innervate the smooth muscle in the artery and arteriole walls. Vasomotor fibers stimulate the smooth muscle cells to constrict , reducing the diameter of the vessels . This process is called vasoconstriction .
Inhibition of this process (inhibition of vasomotor impulses) allows the smooth muscle to relax and the diameter of the vessels increase. This is called vasodilation .

Capillary permeability and arrangement
Capillary permeabilit y- in order for the capillaries to exchange nutrients and wastes with the tissues we must have openings or intercellular channels in the capillary walls. These intercellular channels are simply thin slits amongst the endothelial cells where they overlap. The size of these openings (and therefore the permeability of these capillaries) vary from tissue to tissue.
small openings- muscle tissues (skeletal, cardiac, and smooth)
larger- around endocrine glands, the kidneys, and the lining of the small intestines.
largest- liver, spleen, and red bone marrow
Arrangement- higher the tissue’s rate of metabolism, the denser the capillary network.
Ex. muscles and nerves = high metabolism= many capillary beds
cartilage, epidermis = slower metabolism= fewer capillaries
Regulation of capillary blood flow- The distribution of blood in the various capillary pathways is mainly regulated by smooth muscles that encircle the capillary entrances . These muscles form precapillary sphincters which may close a capillary by contracting or open a capillary by relaxing. These precapillary sphincters respond to cells needs and demands. E.g. cells needs more oxygen and nutrients, precapillary sphincter relaxes, blood flow increases.

Blood pressure
The arterial blood pressure rises and falls in patterns corresponding to the phases of the cardiac cycle.
when ventricles contract , forcing blood into the pulmonic circulation and the aorta, the pressure rises . The max pressure achieved during ventricular contraction is called systolic pressure . When the ventricles relax (diastole), the arterial pressure drops, the lowest pressure that remains before the next contraction is called diastolic pressure .
Pulse - expansion and recoil* of arterial walls following the contraction of the heart. We often use the radial pulse to calculate the heart’s bpm (since the radial pulse rate is equal to left ventricular contraction rate). We can also get a clue as to the blood pressure using the pulse.
high pressure- pulse is strong and full (bounding pulse)
low pressure- pulse is weak (difficult to palpate) and easily compressed
*Without this elastic recoil in the arteries there would be no blood pressure during ventricular diastole. So the elastic recoil of arteries is what maintains the pressure outside the heart.
It also keeps the blood flow smooth and continuous through the capillaries.
places to detect a pulse

Factors that affect arterial pressure
Heart action (SV and HR)- blood pressure will vary with changes in the cardiac output thus if sv or hr increase..so will bp initially..and the same can be said if sv and hr go down, so does cardiac output and therefore bp .
Blood volume- blood pressure is usually directly proportional to the blood volume . Thus, if blood volume increases, so will blood pressure; blood volume decreases, so does blood pressure.
Peripheral resistance (resistance to flow)- this is created by friction between the blood and the walls of the blood vessels . In order for blood to reach its destinations it must overcome peripheral resistance. So if peripheral resistance increases…so will blood pressure. E.g. increase in tone of smooth muscle surrounding arterioles can back up blood in the arteries..increasing the pressure.
Viscosity - physical property of fluid that derives from the ease with which its molecules flow past one another . The greater the viscosity, the greater the resistance to flow. Normally the viscosity is reflective of how many blood cells or plasma proteins are present in the blood. Increase number of cells = increased viscosity = increasde bp ; too few cells = decreased viscosity = decreased bp

Control of blood pressure
BP is controlled by cardiac output (CO) and peripheral resistance (PR)
BP = CO x PR where again CO = SV x HR and SV is calculated by subtracting the volume of blood in the ventricle at the end of a beat (called end-systolic volume) from the volume of blood just prior to the beat (called end-diastolic volume). In a nutshell: if BP is too high we can decease the CO ( cardioinhibitory reflex with parasympathetic) or decrease PR (dilate arteries)
Maintenance of blood pressure, therefore, depends on the maintenance of these two factors.

Further discussion on CO
Again, CO = SV x HR
We mentioned HR can be controlled by braking the heart, releasing the brake, or stimulating with sympathetic supply to Beta adrenergic receptors (these receptors are what beta blockers work on if I need more filling time for the heart)
SV- dependent on several factors
contractility of the heart – forcefulness of the beat, based on sympathetic supply similar to HR
preload - how much volume the heart can contract on. Preload is essentially a reflection of how much the heart tissue is stretched in relation to how much blood has filled the ventricle at EDV (end-diastolic volume). This relationship is explained by the Frank Starling law which states: the more blood that enters from the veins, the greater the distension of the heart, the stronger the contraction, the greater the SV, the greater the CO. Thus, CO is dependent on venous return.
afterload - deals with how much force is needed to overcome peripheral resistance. Increased arterial pressure = increased afterload .
Vasomotor center of the medulla oblongata continually works to make sure the tone of the arteries is compliant with the bodies needs to maintain blood pressure and get blood to the tissues.

Hypertension- high blood pressure
page 588

Measuring the heart beat
ECG or electrocardiogram – measures the electrical changes taking place during the heart’s cycle

Measuring the heart beat
ECG’s give information in the form of deflections on a graph. But what do the deflections indicate?

P wave – indicates atrial depolarization
QRS complex – indicates ventricular depolarization
T wave – ventricular repolarization

Reading ECG’s for problems FYI
1.  Increased time of P-Q (PR) or first degree heart block) - P-R intervals
0.2+ seconds can be due to coronary artery disease (atherosclerosis), rheumatic fever,
or lyme myocarditis - all may result in an increase of scar tissue which slows transmission
between the SA and AV nodes. If the P-R gets longer with successive beats, Type I
Second degree heart block is present: when the p waves must signal twice or more before
the QRS happens, with an increased P-R, Type II Second degree heart block is the
diagnosis. If the SA and AV are disconnected entirely, you have Third degree heart
block.
2.  S-T may be elevated by acute MI.  It may be below the baseline if ischemia
is occurring.  The T wave may be elevated by high K+.  What conditions could cause
high blood K+?
3.  Elevated P waves can indicate enlarged atria.
4.  An elevated R wave can indicate an enlarged ventricle.
5.  Multiple P waves indicate atrial tachycardia.  If they increase even further, atrial
fibrillation is indicated.
6.  Ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation occur as heart rate increases greatly.
The rapid QRS waves obscure the P and T waves.  Blood flow practically ceases.
7.  Premature Ventricular contraction (PVCs) show the QRS "stepping on" the
P wave.

M.I.
Myocardial infarction – the needs of the heart tissue itself is not met. Part of the myocardial wall dies and we call it a heart attack.
If heart becomes damaged severely, such as by MI, the pumping ability of the heart is immediately depressed. As a result, 2 essential effects occur:
(1) reduced CO
(2) back up of blood in veins

S/S of M.I.
Angina
Pain in arms (usually left)
Pain in the shoulder
Pain in the jaw
Feeling of impending doom
Patient describes it as if someone was “standing on chest”
Sweating
Shortness of breath

M.I. and heart regulation
Subsequently, baroreceptors (baro = mmHg) and chemoreceptors note the decreased heart output. Employment of sympathetic nervous system to offset the problem begins. Medical intervention needs to be used to help alleviate the problem as well.
But first we need to diagnose where the problem is…..MI’s may not be the only problem. The heart can also be affected as a result of a combination of problems such as poor diet (atherosclerosis), high BP, diabetes, lung problems, etc…

CHF (Congestive Heart Failure)
May be a result of MI, prolonged hypertension, etc…
CHF- named after the congestion in the lungs that usually accompanies this condition. This problem indicates the heart is having difficulty pumping and blood is backing up “somewhere”.

CHF: Left Heart Failure and Right Heart Failure
Left sided heart failure- left ventricle is unable to pump all the blood due to arterial resistance (high blood pressure). Blood subsequently gets backed up in the lungs.
Right sided heart failure – rt ventricle cannot pump against pulmonic resistance (usually caused by left sided heart failure). Blood subsequently gets backed up in the venous system.
Cor pulmonale – rt sided heart failure without involvement of left sided heart failure. Something other than the left side of the heart is causing the lungs to have greater resistance.
CHF and cor pulmonale

S/S of CHF
Right sided heart failure – pitting edema, swollen abdomen, swollen legs, shortness of breath, jvd, jugular reflex, proxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea.
Left sided heart failure – can have all of rt sided s/s plus cough and sputum production due to edema in the lungs
CHF

Cardiomegaly
Big bag or thick rigid muscle. As we mentioned before, when the heart is damaged or not pumping as much blood as metabolic demand requires, the sympathetic nervous system come to the rescue. Sympathetic involvement can either add more blood to the system (venous constriction) thus increasing SV, increase contractility (again increase SV), or increase HR.
Left or right ventricles over a long period of time get stretched beyond their normal capacity so they either get stronger or more saggy. Either way the heart is less efficient.
Cardiomegaly

AA, VV, Caps, and filtration
Arteries – thick, muscular, carry blood away from heart, strong contractile properties
Veins – larger diameter, less muscular, more distensible than aa, carry blood twd heart.
Capillaries – very small in diameter, many gaps in the walls. Filter out the good, suck in bad

Filtration
Filtration – CHP and ICOP = capillary hystrostatic pressure and interstitial colloidal osmotic pressure. Pushes substance and ions out of capillaries
Reabsorption – CCOP and IHP = capillary colloidal osmotic pressure and interstitial hydrostatic pressure. Pulls substance and ions into capillaries
Problems with this system usually due to kidney problems and loss of proteins.
Filtration
Filtration
Filtration
